coredata.dc:title: Chemical synthesis of dendritic interlaced network graphene quantum dots/sulfur composite for lithium-sulfur batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Le
Yang, Rong
Liu, Rui
Zou, Yiming
Liu, Ying
Chen, Liping
Yan, Yinglin
Xu, Yunhua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, graphene quantum dots/sulfur (GQDs/S) composite was prepared as cathode material for lithium sulfur (Li-S) batteries by a feasible chemical method. Multiple polar functional groups in GQDs can provide more active absorption sites to significantly strengthen the chemical absorption with polysulfides, suppressing the ‘shuttle effect’. In addition, the highly dispersed GQDs particles in the solution served as nucleation sites, the dissolved sulfur are reprecipitated around the GQDs, forming a dendritic interlaced network structure to prevent the aggregation of the sulfur, and further enhance the utilization of active substances. A high initial discharge capacity of 1125 mA h g−1 at 0.1C has been obtained in the GQDs/S cell, which is much higher than the initial discharge capacity of 658 mA h g−1 of GQDs/SR prepared by physical melting method. The superior performance of GQDs/S is attributed to the unique dendritic interlaced framework and multiple polar functional groups. The strong chemical effects between polar functional groups and polysulfides have been confirmed by the absorption experiment and DFT calculation. This work develops a new strategy in cathode material design for Li-S batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium sulfur batteries
Graphene quantum dots
Chemical method
Dendritic interlaced network structure

National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Schematic illustration for the synthesis of GQDs/S composite and application to the sulfur host of Li-S batteries.
(a) TEM, and corresponding size distribution, (b) high-resolution TEM images, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectra of prepared GQDs.

The surface chemistry of GQDs. (a) XPS survey spectrum and (b) corresponding element distribution pie cha of prepared GQDs, the (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, and (e) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the prepared GQDs.
(a) The SEM, (b–c) TEM images of GQDs/S composite, and corresponding elemental mappings of (d) C, (e) S, (f) O, (g) N in the GQDs/S composite.

(a) Charge/discharge profiles for the first, second and fifth cycle at 0.1C of GQDs/S. (b) specific capacity comparison, (c) rate capacity from 0.1C to 1 C, (d) CV curves, (e) cycling performance, and (f) Nyquist plots, of GQDs/S and GQDs/SR cathode.

(a) The comparison of the UV-vis absorption spectra and the inset photo image of the Li2S6 solutions exposed to GQDs adsorbents for 0 h and 12 h, (b) histogram of the calculated binding energies between Li2S and GQDs with different modified groups, the energy density distribution of (c) GQDs, (d) GQDs with -COOH after absorbing.
The charge variation of S, Li, O, H and N atoms before and after absorption between GQDs and Li2S.

head.ce:title: Chemical synthesis of dendritic interlaced network graphene quantum dots/sulfur composite for lithium-sulfur batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, School of Science, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an, 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, School of Science, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an, 710048, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an, 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an, 710048, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yulin University, Yulin, 719000, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, graphene quantum dots/sulfur (GQDs/S) composite was prepared as cathode material for lithium sulfur (Li-S) batteries by a feasible chemical method. Multiple polar functional groups in GQDs can provide more active absorption sites to significantly strengthen the chemical absorption with polysulfides, suppressing the ‘shuttle effect’. In addition, the highly dispersed GQDs particles in the solution served as nucleation sites, the dissolved sulfur are reprecipitated around the GQDs, forming a dendritic interlaced network structure to prevent the aggregation of the sulfur, and further enhance the utilization of active substances. A high initial discharge capacity of 1125 mA h g−1 at 0.1C has been obtained in the GQDs/S cell, which is much higher than the initial discharge capacity of 658 mA h g−1 of GQDs/SR prepared by physical melting method. The superior performance of GQDs/S is attributed to the unique dendritic interlaced framework and multiple polar functional groups. The strong chemical effects between polar functional groups and polysulfides have been confirmed by the absorption experiment and DFT calculation. This work develops a new strategy in cathode material design for Li-S batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium sulfur batteries with theoretical higher specific capacity (1675 mA h g−1) and energy density (2600 Wh kg−1) are one of the most promising alternatives for the next generation secondary batteries [1,2]. However, the severe “shuttle effect” results from the dissolved long-chain polysulfides (Sx 2−, 4 ≤ x ≤ 8), leading to the irreversible loss of active material, poor coulombic efficiency and cycling performance [3,4]. To solve this issue, many strategies have been proposed, such as optimizing the cathode design [5–7], modifying the separator [8,9], and developing the electrolyte [10,11]. Among these possible approaches, carbon-based materials [12–15] as sulfur host play a significant role in immobilizing polysulfides due to the high conductivity, excellent mechanical stability, large surface area and adjustable pore structure. Nevertheless, the relatively weak interaction between nonpolar carbon structure and polar polysulfides can result in the poor electrochemical performance [16]. As previous literatures [17,18], the heteroatoms, such as O, N, B, in the carbon skeleton can strengthen chemical anchoring to polysulfides because the lone electron pair on the exotic atoms exits electrostatic attraction. For example, Shao [19]. et al. synthesized mesoporous Co, N-doped carbon nanosheets, which possessed high specific surface area and affluent defects. The results of DFT calculation and in-situ Raman spectra demonstrate that the Co-N-C catalytic sites as the effective host not only can confine the polysulfides, but also facilitate the conversation from long-chain polysulfides to Li2S2/Li2S, increasing the specific capacity and promoting the reaction kinetics. A high initial specific capacity of 1171 mA h g−1 was derived and maintained at 907.5 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.2C with coulombic efficiency around 100%.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new kind of zero-dimensional honeycomb sp2 carbon nanomaterials, possess the favorable electric conductivity, high specific area, excellent solubility and quantum confinement effect, which have been used as electrode material or additive in the lithium-ion batteries [20,21], supercapacitors [22,23], and fuel cells [24,25].The oxygen-containing groups can be easily introduced into the edge and surface of GQDs through top-down or bottom-up strategies, which can be beneficial to absorb the polysulfides in Li-S batteries. In 2016, Park [26] et al. synthesized GQDs-S/carbon black (CB) cathode material for Li-S batteries. The GQDs ensure the structural integrity via charge interaction with S and CB by oxygen-rich functional groups. The C-S bonds generated during cycling due to the oxygen-rich groups at the edge of GQDs, which reduce the loss of polysulfides.

In this work, GQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups was prepared as cathode material for Li-S batteries by a top-down method. Flake graphite as the starting material was treated by strong acid, the GQDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and a few pyrrole-N was obtained finally. GQDs/S composite was synthesized by a chemical method, where the GQDs served as nucleation sites and surrounded by the reprecipitated sulfur, forming a dendritic interlaced network structure. The branch-liked construction prevents the aggregation of sulfur and improves the utilization of sulfur, further improving the electrochemical performance of Li-S batteries. During the cycling processes, the polar functional groups of GQDs can help to preserve dissolved polysulfides by chemical effect, which can effectively relieve shuttle effect. The results have been confirmed by Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Graphite (325 mesh) was brought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, the concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) were brought by Tianjin tianli chemical reagent factory. N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent was purchased from Aladdin Shanghai Co. Ltd. The electrolyte of Li-bis (trifluoromethanesulfonyl) mide (LiTFSI, 1 M) in 1, 3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1, 2-dimethoxyethane (DME, v: v = 1:1), Li2S were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co., Ltd.

GQDs was prepared following a chemical oxidation according to reference [27]. NaNO3 (33.3 g) and graphite (1 g) were added to the concentrated H2SO4 (100 mL) with stirring for 40 min in ice bath, and then KMnO4 (3 g) was added to the above solution with stirring for 20 min. The obtained graphene oxide slurry was heated at 40 °C for 1 h, and then transferred into three-necked flask to reflux at 120 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the solution was diluted with deionized water (300 mL) and added Na2CO3 to adjust its pH to 3. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed (1-kDa molar mass cut off) for 3 days. Finally, the GQDs solution was obtained and preserved at 4 °C.

Sulfur powder (0.12 g) was dissolved in toluene solution (15 mL), and then GQDs (60 mL) and ethanol (50 mL) were added into the solution under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the mixture solution was heated at 50 °C to precipitate sulfur. Finally, the concentrated solution was dried at 50 °C to obtain GQDs/S composite.
For comparison, sulfur was mixed with GQDs, and then heated at 158 °C for 12 h, which is referred as GQDs/SR.

For the polysulfide absorbency test, Li2S6 solution was prepared. Lithium sulfide (Li2S) and sulfur powder at a molar ration of 1:5 were dissolved into 7 mL of 1,3-dioxolane and 1,2-dimethoxyethane (1:1 by volume). After that, the resulting solution was stirred at 50 °C for 48 h, and then 1 mL Li2S6 solution was diluted into 0.005 M in a sample vial with 0.015 g GQDs for the static absorption experiment. The color change was recorded using a digital camera in the same interval. The operations were completed in a glove box filled with argon atmosphere.

The black slurry was obtained by mixing GQDs/S, conductive carbon (KS-6), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) at a weight ratio of 7:2:1 in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) with vigorous stirring for 24 h. Then, the slurry was coated on Al foil and dried at 55 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The CR2025 coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box. Lithium metal, GQDs/S and polypropylene membrane (celgard 2320) were used as the anode, cathode, and separator, respectively. Li-bis (trifluoromethanesulfonyl)mide (LiTFSI, 1 M) in 1, 3-dioxolane (DOL) and 1, 2-dimethoxyethane (DME) (1/1, v/v) was used as electrolyte. The GQDs/SR electrode was prepared in the same way.

The morphologies of GQDs and GQDs/S were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, VEGA-3-SBU-EasyProb) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-3010). The properties of GQDs and GQDs/S were obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA), raman spectra (Invia), fluorescence spectrometer (Rf-6000), ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-vis, WFH-203), fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-S900), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, JW-BK122W) and powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6100).

The electrochemical tests were recorded on a CHI 650D electrochemical workstation. The galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements were carried out under a voltage window of 1.5–3.0 V (versus Li/Li+) at different current density. The cyclic voltammograms (CV) was conducted between 1.0 and 3.5 V (versus Li/Li+) at a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were used from 0.01 Hz to 1 MHz at the amplitude of 5 mV.

The synthetic process of GQDs/S composite is illustrated in Fig. 1 . By using a top-down strategy, the GQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups was obtained after acid oxidation treatment. Subsequently, the GQDs/S was synthesized by a chemical method due to the good dispersibility of GQDs in solution. With the evaporation of solution, the dissolved sulfur was reprecipitated around the GQDs, and grew in different directions, forming a dendritic interlaced network structure. GQDs/S composite with the unique structure acts as the cathode material for Li-S batteries. During the discharge process, the long-chain polysulfides are anchored on the polar functional groups (-COOH, -OH) of GQDs to suppress the dissolution of long-chain polysulfides into the electrolyte, and further improve the cycling performance of Li-S batteries.

The photoluminescence properties of GQDs were evaluated as shown in Fig. S1. The UV-vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs (black curve) shows a typical π→π∗ absorption band of the graphitic sp2 at 239 nm and n→π∗ transition peak at 342 nm, implying that the prepared GQDs with polar groups [28]. A brighter blue fluorescence can be observed under irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp. In addition, the intensity of PL spectrum increases and then reduces with the excitation wavelength varied from 420 nm to 500 nm, shows excitation wavelength-dependent properties originate from heterogeneous groups of prepared GQDs consistent with reports in the literature [29].

The internal structure of GQDs was detailed in the TEM images in Fig. 2 a. The particles of GQDs present uniform distribution and average diameters about 2.41 nm in size (inset Fig. 2a.). The high resolution TEM in Fig. 2b displays a clear interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) lattice fringes of graphene [30]. The phase of GQDs was identified by XRD in Fig. 2c. The diffraction peak around at 22° originates from the (002) plane of graphite [31], confirming the graphitized structure of GQDs, which can facilitate to increase the electrical conductivity and diffusion of ions/electrons in the GQDs/S cell. Raman spectra of GQDs is shown in Fig. 2d. The ratio of integrated intensity D to G band (ID/IG) represents the degree of defects. The ID/IG value of GQDs is 0.845, which is slightly higher than graphite [32], indicating that defects have been introduced into the edge of GQDs while the sp2 C structure is retained. The sp2 C can be conducive to improving the electrical conductivity and rapid charge transfer in the cell. In Fig. S2, the FTIR spectra was explored the functional groups of GQDs. The characteristic O-H and hydrogen bond stretching absorption are located at 3443 cm−1 and the N-H stretching vibration is presented at 3136 cm−1. The bands at 1398 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1 are due to the C-H and the asymmetric stretching vibration of C=O, respectively, indicating the carboxyl and hydroxyl simultaneously exist in GQDs. In addition, a weak peak is observed that is assigned to the C-N band [33].

To further investigate the chemical states of GQDs, XPS measurement was performed, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The C 1s, O 1s and N 1s with 53.87 wt%, 43.88 wt% and 1.89 wt% are detected in Fig. 3b, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s in Fig. 3c reveals the existence of sp2 and sp3 C. The O 1s peaks in Fig. 3d are presented by the states of C-O (531.95 eV), C=O (533.71. eV) and O-C=O (536.47 eV), confirming the presence of oxygen-containing groups in GQDs. The pyrrole-N peak has been detected at 398.75 eV in the spectra of N 1s of Fig. 3e [34,35]. Hence, the rich oxygen- and N-containing functional groups in the prepared GQDs can efficiently absorb the polysulfides, which can be discussed by the adsorption experiment and first principle calculations in detail [36].

The complex between sulfur and GQDs are further proofed by XRD in Fig. S3a. It can be clearly observed that the peaks of the obtained GQDs/S composite are matched well with the characteristic diffraction peaks of elemental sulfur, indicating a successful combination. The content of sulfur is around 59 wt% in the GQDs/S and GQDs/SR composites, which is calculated according to the TG curves (Fig. S3b.). The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of GQDs shows a type-Ⅲ curve with the specific surface area of 31.3 m2/g as shown in Fig. S3c. The pore size distribution shows mesopores with 5 nm in diameter, which is caused by stacking of GQDs layers. After the load of sulfur, the specific surface area and pore size are dramatically decreased to 2.6 m2/g and 3 nm, indicating that a small portion of sulfur is embedded in the pores.

The morphology of GQDs/S presents a blocky structure as shown in Fig. 4 a. In addition, the uniform distribution of C, S, O and N elements in the GQDs/S composite was confirmed by spectroscopy maps with energy dispersive X-rays (EDS), as shown in Fig. 4d–g. In addition, GQDs/S with a dendritic interconnected framework can be observed in TEM image of Fig. 4b–c. Compared with other synthetic methods, a feasible and scalable strategy has been applied in this work, that well-dispersed GQDs particles served as the nucleation are surrounded by the reprecipitated sulfur, and then form the dendritic structure, preventing the aggregation of sulfur.

The electrochemical performance of GQDs/S and GQDs/SR were investigated in Fig. 5 . The galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of GQDs/S and GQDs/SR were performed within the cut-off voltage of 1.5–3.0 V at 0.1C (Fig. 5a, Fig. S4). An initial discharge capacity of 1125 mA h g−1 is derived in GQDs/S cell, which is higher than the capacity of 658 mA h g−1 in GQDs/SR cell, implying the improvement of sulfur utilization due to the good dispersion of sulfur in the dendritic network structure of GQDs/S composite. Predictably, the specific capacity of GQDs/S invariably outclass GQDs/SR cathode at the second, and fifth cycles in Fig. 5b. At the current density gradually increase from 0.1C to 1 C, and then decrease to 0.1C, the discharge capacities of GQDs/S are 907, 541, 344, 230, and 456 mA h g−1, respectively, indicating a superior rate capability and recovery capability. In contrast, the poor rate capability of the GQDs/SR electrode has shown in Fig. 5c, especially, at high current density. CV measurement was used to provide information of electron transfer in the charge/discharge process. At a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1, the typical CV curves of GQDs/S and GQDs/SR electrodes have been shown in Fig. 5d. Two representative reduction peaks located at ∼1.9 V and ∼2.3 V, corresponding to the transformation of S8 to long-chain polysulfides and further to Li2S2/Li2S. One anodic peak at ∼2.8 V is identified as the oxidation process from Li2S2/Li2S to S8. In comparison to GQDs/SR electrode, a tiny positive shift of cathodic peaks and negative shift of anodic peak of GQDs/S electrode are observed, suggesting that the reaction kinetics has been enhanced owing to the unique the dendric framework structure. Due to the innovative loading sulfur method of GQDs/S, a high discharge capacity retention of 344 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.1C are maintained, while the discharge capacity retention of GQDs/SR is only 216 mA h g−1 as shown in Fig. 5e. EIS measurements of GQDs/S and GQDs/SR cells before cycling are depicted in Fig. 5f. The Nyquist plots are consisting of a semicircle in the high frequency region representing the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and an oblique line located in low frequency corresponding to Warburg impedance. Rct of GQDs/SR cell is approximately 415.7 Ω, which is 3.5 times higher than GQDs/SR cell, due to the fast reaction kinetics in the GQDs/S cell.

To directly examine the absorbency of GQDs for polysulfides, GQDs was immersed in Li2S6 solution (inset Fig. 6 a). The typical yellow color of Li2S6 solution with GQDs is gradually faded to light-yellow color after 12 h. To further confirm the absorbability of GQDs to the polysulfides, the supernatant of GQDs-containing and pure Li2S6 solution were evaluated by UV-vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 6a.). The adsorption signals occur in the wavelength region of 220–320 nm, and the intensity of GQDs-containing solution is lower than pure Li2S6 solution, implying that GQDs can capture polysulfides effectively [37].

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations using Materials Studio Dmol3 model were carried out to reveal the interaction between the polysulfides and polar functional groups in GQDs. Li2S molecule acts as the representative of polysulfides to illustrate the binding interaction between GQDs and polysulfides. The optimized structures of Li2S and the individual functional group in GQDs (-COOH, -OH, -C=O, pyrrole-N) are shown in Fig. S5, respectively. According to the previous literatures [38], the value of the binding energy is proportional to the strength of the chemical interaction between materials and polysulfides. In Fig. 6b, the binding energy values of GQDs with different functional groups are −4.02 eV, −4.53 eV, −2.7 eV, and −3.06 eV towards -OH, -COOH, -C=O, and pyrrole-N, respectively, indicating there is strong chemical effects between polar groups and Li2S, especially the functional group of -COOH. The distribution of electron density between the Li2S and GQDs, Li2S and GQDs with -COOH are shown in Fig. 6c–d, respectively. Under the isovalue of 0.1, the electron density of Li2S shows partial overlap towards GQDs while a striking overlap of electron density between Li2S and GQDs with -COOH, which reveals the strong chemical interaction between -COOH and polysulfides. Meanwhile, the electron density distribution of GQDs with -OH, -C=O, pyrrole-N and Li2S are coincided in Fig. S6. A overlap can be observed clearly, which indicates it is feasible to absorb polysulfides by GQDs in Li-S batteries.

Simultaneously, the charge of Li, S, N, O are also changed before and after absorption in Table 1 , indicating the rebuilding of chemical bonds between Li2S and polar groups of GQDs to effectively reduce the ‘shuttle effect’. In addition, -COOH with the strongest binding energies among these groups in GQDs is attributed to a double negatively charged centers of O atoms served as absorption center and provided absorption sites for Li+ with positive charge. Hence, it is proved that GQDs can alleviate the shuttle effect due to the strongly chemical interaction.

In summary, GQDs/S composite with the unique dendric interconnected network is obtained as cathode material for Li-S batteries, which can alleviate the ‘shuttle effect’ by chemical adsorption. Rich oxygen- and N-containing functional groups in GQDs/S are used as polar sites to absorb the polysulfides. In comparison with the physical melting method, the peculiar chemical method used to load sulfur can prevent the agglomeration. The special branch-liked network structure significantly improves the utilization of sulfur, so that the GQDs/S electrode delivers an initial specific capacity of 1125 mA h g−1 at 0.1C and possesses a lower charge transfer resistance. The strong chemical effect between the polar groups of GQDs and polysulfides, especially in -COOH groups, has been confirmed by the DFT calculation. Our work shows that the GQDs served as the sulfur host have achieved tentative success in improving the electrochemical performance of Li-S batteries. Incorporating GQDs with other materials to play a synergetic effect should be attracted more attention in the design of electrode materials.

Le Gong: Data curation, Thoughts organization, Writing-manuscripts, Disposal data, Visualization. Rong Yang: Project administration, project management, Resources, and laboratory, Writing - review & editing. Rui Liu: Investigation, Visualization, DFT calculations, modeling program. Yiming Zou: Data curation, Data administration. Ying Liu: Validation. Liping Chen: Writing - review & editing. Yinglin Yan: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by International Cooperation Project (2015DFR50350) funded by Ministry of Science and Technology of China, Youth Project (51702256) funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China, General Programs (2019M653706, 2018M633544) funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation, Key Programs (2019TD-019, 2020JM450) funded by Science and Technology Department of Shannxi Provincial Government, Natural and Scientific Programs (18JK0579) funded by Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.157278.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smaller sulfur molecules promise better lithium-sulfur batteries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast conversion and controlled deposition of lithium (poly)sulfides in lithium-sulfur batteries using high-loading cobalt single atoms

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Li, J.B. Wu, B. Zhang, W.Y. Wang, G.Q. Zhang, Z.W. She, N. Zhang, J. Sun, L. Huang, J.J. Jiang, J. Zhou, Y.M. Sun, Fast conversion and controlled deposition of lithium (poly)sulfides in lithium-sulfur batteries using high-loading cobalt single atoms, Energy Storage Mater. 30 (2020) 250-259.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hollow graphene spheres coated separator as an efficient trap for soluble polysulfides in Li-S battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.P. Wang, J. Zhang, Y.X. Yin, H. Duan, J. Chou, S.Y. Li, M. Yan, S. Xin, Y.G. Guo, A rational reconfiguration of electrolyte for high-energy and long-life lithium-chalcogen batteries, Adv. Mater. (2020) 2000302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel permselective organo-polysulfides/PVDF gel polymer electrolyte enables stable lithium anode for lithium-sulfur batteries
